articles.title: Top CBRE Researcher Ebere Anokute: E-Commerce’s Impact Is ‘Overblown’
articles.narrative: CBRE's head of retail research for the Americas, Ebere Anokute, argues that the impact of e-commerce on physical retail is often overstated, with brick-and-mortar stores still accounting for the vast majority of retail sales. He highlights that the retail market is currently experiencing a positive moment due to a supply-constrained environment and outsized demand, particularly from service tenants, grocery stores, and fitness concepts. Anokute points to the Sun Belt region, including cities like Dallas, Phoenix, Houston, and various parts of Florida, as outperforming markets driven by population growth and new retail construction. He emphasizes consumer spending as a leading indicator for physical retail demand and notes the growth of discount retailers in response to economic uncertainty, while also stressing that 'value' doesn't always equate to 'cheap'.

people.name: Ebere Anokute
people.title: Head of Retail Research for the Americas

articles.title: Gersh Autism Academy Signs 21K-SF Bronx Sublease at 425 College Avenue
articles.narrative: Gersh Autism Academy, a nonprofit serving autistic children, has subleased a 21,000-square-foot building at 452 College Avenue in the Mott Haven neighborhood of the Bronx. The space was formerly occupied by Brilla Schools, which is consolidating its operations. The transaction reflects strong demand for educational facilities in the area.

data_points.address: 452 College Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: Brilla Schools closed this location to move students to a new facility at 420 East 145th Street.

articles.title: Design Firm GKV Architects Signs 6K-SF Lease at AmTrustRE’s 360 Lexington Avenue
articles.narrative: GKV Architects, an architecture and interior design firm, has signed a 5,754-square-foot lease for a portion of the 14th floor at 360 Lexington Avenue in Midtown East, relocating from its previous offices at 675 Third Avenue. The building, acquired by AmTrustRE in 2024, is seeing active leasing, with the landlord noting GKV's prior collaboration on several of its projects. This lease follows other recent transactions at the property, indicating strong tenant interest in the repositioned asset.

data_points.address: 360 Lexington Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: AmTrustRE and Capstone Equities acquired the 24-story office tower in September 2024 for $65 million.

people.name: Anne Holker
people.title: Managing Director of Leasing and Acquisitions

articles.market_intelligence: The average office asking rent in Midtown in March was $85.11 per square foot.

articles.title: Publix Buys Another South Florida Retail Center
articles.narrative: Publix has acquired the Fountains of Boynton Shopping Center, a 178,726-square-foot retail property in Boynton Beach, FL, for $78 million. The grocery chain is the anchor tenant at the center, which it purchased from a partnership between Bolder Group and Union Investment Real Estate. This acquisition is part of Publix's strategy to buy properties it occupies, a trend observed among retailers in the current high-interest-rate environment.

data_points.address: 6661 Boynton Beach Boulevard, Boynton Beach, FL
data_points.notable_features: Open-air retail property adjacent to South Jog Road.
data_points.project_notes: The seller previously acquired the asset for $79.5 million in 2021.

articles.market_intelligence: Publix has spent over $400 million since 2024 acquiring real estate it occupies. In December, Publix purchased a 137,780-square-foot retail center in Boca Raton for $83 million from Jamestown. Two months prior, it acquired a shopping center near Kendall for $72 million.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Expands to 41K SF at AmTrustRE’s 260 Madison Avenue
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap has expanded its footprint at 260 Madison Avenue in Midtown Manhattan, adding 5,000 square feet to its existing 36,000-square-foot office space, bringing its total to 41,000 square feet. The real estate advisory firm also extended its lease at the property, which is owned by AmTrustRE. The landlord noted its commitment to investing in and repositioning New York City office assets, citing the retention of a high-quality tenant like Marcus & Millichap as a testament to its strategy.

data_points.address: 260 Madison Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: 22-story office building near Grand Central Terminal.
data_points.project_notes: AmTrustRE acquired the building in October 2025 for $217 million. Marcus & Millichap originally signed a 10-year lease in 2015.

articles.market_intelligence: The average office asking rent in Midtown was $85.11 per square foot in March.

articles.title: JPI Begins Construction on 277-Unit Multifamily Project in The Colony, Texas
articles.narrative: JPI has commenced construction on Jefferson Grandscape II, a 277-unit multifamily development in The Colony, Texas. The $77.8 million project, located within the larger Grandscape mixed-use development, will feature a five-story building with a diverse unit mix and amenities such as a pool and sky lounge. QuadReal Property Group and Nebraska Furniture Mart are providing construction financing, with initial units anticipated for occupancy in early 2028.

data_points.notable_features: Located within the 433-acre Grandscape mixed-use development; amenities include a pool, sky lounge, and outdoor courtyards with fire pits.

articles.title: CapRock Partners Breaks Ground on 250,000 SF Industrial Project in McKinney, Texas
articles.narrative: CapRock Partners has commenced construction on McKinney Air Business Park, a two-building industrial development totaling 250,000 square feet in McKinney, Texas. The shallow-bay project, situated on a 15.3-acre site adjacent to McKinney National Airport, is expected to be completed in early 2027. Colliers is handling the leasing efforts for the property.

data_points.notable_features: Two-building, shallow-bay development with 32-foot clear heights, ESFR sprinkler systems, ample dock-high loading, and 130-foot truck court depths, adjacent to McKinney National Airport.

articles.title: Eastham Capital Sells 194-Unit Apartment Complex in Southeast Houston
articles.narrative: Eastham Capital has sold University Green Apartments, a 194-unit multifamily complex in southeast Houston. The property was approximately 97 percent occupied at the time of sale. Eastham Capital had acquired the asset in 2018 in a joint venture with Mosaic Residential and implemented capital improvements during its ownership.

data_points.notable_features: Amenities include a pool, fitness center, and a dog park.
data_points.project_notes: Eastham Capital acquired the asset in 2018 in a joint venture with Mosaic Residential and subsequently implemented capital improvements.

articles.title: CRC Provides $6.1M HUD-Insured Loan for Refinancing of Metro Dallas Memory Care Facility
articles.narrative: Cambridge Realty Capital (CRC) has provided a $6.1 million HUD-insured loan for the refinancing of Avalon Memory Care Keller, a 50-bed memory care facility located in Keller, Texas. The financing was originated through HUD's 223(f) program on behalf of the undisclosed owner.

data_points.notable_features: Standalone facility for patients with Alzheimer's and dementia.
data_points.project_notes: HUD 223(f) program loan.

articles.title: US Merchants Buys 453,960 SF Industrial Facility in Surprise, Arizona
articles.narrative: US Merchants has acquired Building One at West Summit at Surprise, a 453,960-square-foot industrial facility in Surprise, Arizona, from developer Mohr Capital for $63.9 million. The property, completed in the third quarter of 2024, is part of a larger two-building industrial park and features modern amenities suitable for logistics and distribution operations.

data_points.property_name: Building One at West Summit at Surprise
data_points.notable_features: Features 36-foot clear height, LED lighting, a 2,951-square-foot speculative office suite, full air conditioning, 3,000 amps of expandable power, 60-foot speed bays, a 145-foot secured concrete truck court, 101 dock doors, and four grade-level doors.

articles.title: Lawrence Group transforming 1800s train depot into retail incubator in Buda
articles.narrative: Lawrence Group, in partnership with Logic Builders Group, is transforming a historic 1800s train depot in Buda, Texas, into "The Depot on Main," a 2,000-square-foot retail incubator and community marketplace. This design-build project aims to foster small businesses and local products, serving as a launchpad for entrepreneurs and a community hub. The redevelopment, which received Buda City Council approval in March, involves relocating the original depot building to the Downtown Buda Greenbelt and is expected to be fully completed in early 2027, with portions of the plaza potentially opening by December 2026.

data_points.notable_features: Historic train depot from the late 1800s, designed to maintain original architectural details while providing flexible retail incubator suites, a co-op marketplace, and an enhanced exterior plaza with an event lawn and performance stage.
data_points.project_notes: The original train depot building was removed in the early 1980s to private land and relocated in November 2025 to the Downtown Buda Greenbelt as part of this redevelopment project. A phased activation approach may allow portions of the plaza to open as early as December 2026.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap: A bright future still ahead for Detroit’s industrial sector
articles.narrative: Detroit's industrial market is currently facing short-term challenges, including elevated vacancies and softer pricing, but is buoyed by its strong manufacturing heritage and significant corporate investments. Major manufacturers are injecting capital into advanced manufacturing and battery production, creating new opportunities despite ongoing restructuring in the auto industry. The Interstate-96 Corridor is a standout performer, experiencing robust tenant demand and investor interest, while a projected slowdown in new construction is expected to help stabilize the market. Despite a forecasted decline in average asking rents for 2026, the market is anticipated to maintain one of the highest industrial rent averages in the Midwest, positioning it for long-term stability and recovery.
articles.market_intelligence: The Interstate-96 Corridor in Detroit is outperforming the broader market, with tenant demand more than doubling the activity levels seen elsewhere. Investor interest is concentrated in Troy and around Detroit Metropolitan Airport. Oakland County is expected to account for much of the new industrial supply reaching the market in 2026.

articles.title: Gerdom Realty and Investment helps bring fitness center to Novi
articles.narrative: OrangeTheory Fitness has secured a 4,800-square-foot lease at Novi 10 Plaza in Novi, Michigan. The fitness center will occupy the former Hallmark space within the Busch's Grocery-anchored retail center, enhancing the tenant mix at this well-located property.

data_points.notable_features: Grocery-anchored retail center.

articles.title: ULI Austin awards housing pioneer with its annual Vision Award
articles.narrative: ULI Austin has honored David Steinwedell, founder of the Texas Housing Conservancy, with its annual Vision Award, recognizing his lifetime contributions to the Austin region's real estate industry and community. Steinwedell, a 45-year veteran of commercial real estate, was instrumental in creating the Austin Housing Conservancy Fund, now the Texas Housing Conservancy, which pioneered a social impact fund model to preserve workforce multifamily housing. This initiative has invested over $500 million in nearly 3,000 units across seventeen properties, aiming to preserve 15,000 units within a decade. His work has been lauded for advancing a sustainable, market-driven approach to housing affordability and economic resilience in growing cities.

data_points.project_notes: The Texas Housing Conservancy Fund has invested over $500 million in moderate-income multifamily housing across seventeen properties, totaling almost 3,000 units.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: National Association of Real Estate Investment Managers (NAREIM)

people.name: David Steinwedell
people.title: founder of the Austin Housing Conservancy Fund and Affordable Central Texas, now Texas Housing Conservancy

articles.title: MAG Capital Partners acquires two temperature-controlled facilities from FreshEdge
articles.narrative: MAG Capital Partners has acquired two temperature-controlled industrial facilities from FreshEdge in a sale-leaseback transaction. The portfolio includes FreshEdge's 104,114-square-foot headquarters in Indianapolis and a 28,800-square-foot warehouse also in Indianapolis. This transaction allows FreshEdge to monetize its real estate while continuing operations under a lease agreement.

data_points.notable_features: The Indianapolis headquarters facility features 23'-34' clear height, 39 dock doors, and one drive-in door, situated along I-70. The second warehouse has 21' clear height and four drive-in doors, located minutes from I-465.
data_points.project_notes: The transaction was structured as a sale-leaseback.

articles.title: Centre Square bidding war erupts as NYC developer scoops local offer
articles.narrative: A bidding war has erupted for Centre Square, Philadelphia's largest office property, which is currently in foreclosure. New York-based CSC Coliving submitted an $80 million bid to acquire the 1.76 million-square-foot complex, surpassing a previous $70 million agreement by local developers Dean Adler and PMC Property Group. Another developer, Universal Group, has also intervened in the foreclosure case, alleging its $75 million all-cash offer was improperly rejected. The property, which last traded for $328 million in 2017, has struggled with high vacancy, and bidders are proposing adaptive reuse plans including residential, hotel, and retail conversions.

data_points.notable_features: Comprises a 36-story East Tower and a 43-story West Tower, located across from City Hall.
data_points.project_notes: The property is in foreclosure, with approximately $375 million remaining on its CMBS loan. It last traded for $328 million in 2017.

articles.title: Housing Notes: IPhones replace boomer vices, but not drive for home buying
articles.narrative: This opinion piece by Jonathan Miller explores the generational differences in housing market motivations, noting that while younger generations are more financially prudent than their Boomer predecessors, they face unprecedented housing affordability challenges. Miller argues that the Federal Reserve's prolonged low interest rates depleted housing inventory, leading to continued price increases despite higher mortgage rates. He proposes several macro-level solutions to address affordability, including zoning reform to increase density, legalizing accessory dwelling units, streamlining permitting processes, utilizing public land and air rights for affordable housing, supporting industrialized construction methods, and implementing land value taxation to discourage speculation.

articles.title: Housing bill sails through House with bipartisan support
articles.narrative: The House of Representatives has passed the amended 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act with bipartisan support, moving a housing affordability bill closer to becoming law. A key change in the House version, which received White House approval, removes a controversial provision that would have required build-to-rent investors to sell rental homes within seven years. This amendment is expected to provide comfort to the build-to-rent industry, which had seen investment frozen due to the previous mandate. The bill also narrows the definition of a single-family home to exclude manufactured housing and renovated homes for sale, drawing support from industry groups like the National Association of Home Builders and the National Association of Realtors.

people.name: Shannon McGahn
people.title: Executive Vice President and Chief Advocacy Officer

articles.title: Elliman’s weak first-quarter performance casts doubt on turnaround
articles.narrative: Douglas Elliman reported a $16 million net loss in the first quarter of 2026, reversing a brief return to profitability in late 2025. The loss was primarily driven by a 15% year-over-year decline in revenue, attributed to fewer home sales and a slowdown in its development marketing division. Despite efforts to reduce operating expenses, the firm faced increased costs from litigation and agent commissions, highlighting ongoing challenges in its recovery from previous losses and leadership changes. The company's new development marketing pipeline, valued at $25 billion, has yet to significantly translate into earnings, with commissions from this sector declining in the first quarter.

articles.title: The Daily Dirt: The life and death of a landlord
articles.narrative: This article reports on the sale of a 67-unit rent-stabilized building in the Bronx, which traded for $3.965 million, or $59,000 per unit. The property was part of a 20-building portfolio being sold by the estate of the late Howard Alkoff, who had owned the building for 42 years. The sale highlights the challenges faced by owners of rent-stabilized properties in New York City following the 2019 Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act, which has impacted revenue and expenses, leading to low valuations despite the building being well-maintained.

data_points.address: 1181 Sheridan Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Well-maintained building with steel-and-glass entrance doors, tiled entryway, massive lobby, steel and marble staircases, clean common areas, dual elevators, and a silver-coated roof in good condition.
data_points.project_notes: The property was part of a 20-building portfolio owned by Howard Alkoff's H&H Equities, which had owned the building for 42 years. The portfolio had a low number of HPD violations compared to distressed buildings. The property's tax bills increased significantly from 2021 to 2024, and H&H Equities fell behind on payments, owing $314,000 in August 2024 before reaching a payment plan.

articles.market_intelligence: The Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act of 2019 has negatively impacted the financial picture for rent-stabilized buildings in New York City, leading to low revenue and rising expenses. The sale price for 1181 Sheridan Avenue was 3.8 times the rent roll, yielding a cap rate of 10.4 percent.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: Sioni Group closes on Garment District property for $18M
articles.narrative: Sioni Group has acquired a vacant 10,300-square-foot commercial building in New York City's Garment District for $18 million. The buyer plans to redevelop the site into a 32-story mixed-use property. The transaction highlights continued investor interest in prime development sites within the city.

data_points.address: 28 West 37th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Vacant commercial building slated for redevelopment into a 32-story mixed-use property.

articles.title: PolicyPro: Upstate rent reg bill builds momentum, COPA secures Council support
articles.narrative: A bill known as the Rent Emergency Stabilization for Tenants (REST) Act is gaining momentum in the New York State Assembly, aiming to expand rent regulation beyond New York City. The proposed legislation, which has cleared two Assembly committees, would allow localities to declare a housing emergency using publicly available data, rather than requiring a vacancy study. It would also lower the threshold for regulated buildings to four units or more and include buildings substantially rehabilitated more than 15 years ago. Meanwhile, the Community Opportunity to Purchase Act (COPA) has secured majority support in the New York City Council, with 26 sponsors, making its passage likely if it reaches a floor vote.

companies_people.label: OTHER
companies_people.firm_name: Homeowners For An Affordable New York

articles.title: Thakkars claim Safehold cut them out of office-to-resi conversion
articles.narrative: The Texas-based owners of a Midtown office tower are suing their ground landlord, Safehold, alleging wrongful termination of their 104-year ground lease at 135 West 50th Street. Thakkar Developers claims Safehold attempted to cut them out of a planned office-to-residential conversion after they brought in MetroLoft as a development partner. The lawsuit seeks to block the lease termination and demands over $500 million in damages, following Safehold's own suit to eject the Thakkars over alleged unpaid property taxes.

data_points.address: 135 West 50th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: 23-story building with planned office-to-residential conversion.
data_points.project_notes: Thakkar Developers acquired the leasehold for $8.5 million in 2024 via an online auction. Safehold filed an application in May to add 32 apartments at the property.

articles.title: Growing law firm leases keep Manhattan’s office market humming
articles.narrative: Manhattan's office market continues to be significantly influenced by law firm activity, with two major firms recently expanding their footprints. Willkie Farr & Gallagher added 53,000 square feet at 787 Seventh Avenue, increasing its total occupancy to 368,000 square feet. Concurrently, Kirkland & Ellis expanded its space at 900 Third Avenue by 52,000 square feet, bringing its total to over 309,000 square feet. These expansions highlight a trend of law firms renewing and growing their presence in the market, as evidenced by a recent Savills report indicating that 70% of first-quarter law firm leasing activity in Manhattan involved renewals or expansions.

data_points.address: 787 Seventh Avenue, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: Willkie Farr & Gallagher's total occupancy at the property is now 368,000 square feet. Kirkland & Ellis's total occupancy at 900 Third Avenue is now over 309,000 square feet.

articles.title: $85M Manalapan mansion with tunnel to the ocean finds buyer
articles.narrative: A sprawling lake-to-ocean estate in Manalapan, featuring a unique tunnel to the beach, has found a buyer. The 22,900-square-foot mansion, owned by Cindy and Ron McMackin of Pan-Pacific Mechanical, was built in 2020 and includes seven bedrooms and 11.5 bathrooms. The property was listed for $85 million and is notable for its direct, air-conditioned tunnel access to the ocean, a rare feature in the area.

data_points.notable_features: Lake-to-ocean estate with 165 feet of ocean frontage, a pool, spa, wellness wing, eight-car garage, guest apartment, and a discreet, air-conditioned tunnel to the beach.
data_points.project_notes: The sellers previously acquired the property for $38.9 million in 2020.

articles.title: Riviera Beach delays vote on impact fee hike after Gold Coast Builders challenge
articles.narrative: Riviera Beach officials have postponed a vote on a proposed 300% increase in development impact fees after the Gold Coast Builders Association raised concerns about the legality and potential negative impact on development. The city council deferred the decision to allow staff to meet with the builders' association, which argues that the proposed fees may not comply with a 2024 state law requiring inter-county agreements for transportation impact fees. City officials contend that the fee hike is necessary to fund infrastructure and services for an anticipated 4,500 new residential units by 2040, but the city attorney expressed uncertainty about the legal defensibility of the proposed fees.

articles.title: Penn-Florida sets stage for Mandarin Oriental hotel dev site auction
articles.narrative: Penn-Florida Companies is preparing for an auction of its Mandarin Oriental hotel development site in Boca Raton, FL, although the firm is still pursuing a recapitalization or refinancing to avoid the sale. The 1-acre site, where a 12-story, 164-key hotel is under construction and structurally complete, is subject to Chapter 11 reorganization filings by Penn-Florida affiliates. The primary creditor is owed $130.2 million, with additional unsecured and secured claims totaling over $100 million.

data_points.address: 103 East Camino Real, Boca Raton, FL
data_points.notable_features: The hotel is topped off and structurally completed.
data_points.project_notes: The site is part of a larger development that also includes an 88-unit condo building at 105 East Camino Real. Penn-Florida affiliates Via Mizner Owner II and Via Mizner Pledgor II filed for Chapter 11 reorganization in December.

articles.title: Related Group scores rezoning for 20-story apartments in busy Riviera Beach
articles.narrative: The Related Group and its partners secured zoning approval for Gallery at Marina Village, a 20-story, 418-unit market-rate apartment project in Riviera Beach. The development, which includes retail and office space, is part of the larger Marina Village redevelopment. The project is situated on a 2.5-acre site leased from the Riviera Beach Community Redevelopment Agency.

data_points.notable_features: The project will include a nine-level parking garage, 3,300 square feet of retail, and 3,000 square feet of offices facing the U.S. 1/Broadway thoroughfare.
data_points.project_notes: The developers secured a 99-year lease for the site in June 2023. The project is part of the 80-acre Marina Village redevelopment area. The city's planning and zoning board had previously pushed for workforce housing at this project, but it was not included in the rezoning documents.

articles.title: “Tampa we love”: Jon Paul, Nick Pérez say some investors “not comfortable” with gulfside city
articles.market: Tampa, FL / West Palm Beach, FL
articles.narrative: Related Group executives Jon Paul Pérez and Nick Pérez discussed the challenges of attracting international investors to Tampa, despite strong job growth and competitive rents. They noted that while luxury home sales and rentals are robust, the broader housing market is flat due to high property taxes and insurance. The firm remains a significant developer in the Tampa Bay market, with billions invested in projects like the Ritz-Carlton Residences. The executives also highlighted a shift towards domestic buyers in South Florida, particularly from the Northeast, Midwest, and West Coast, driven by tax considerations. They emphasized the growing trend of branded and wellness-focused condo developments, with plans for a new wellness-branded project. West Palm Beach was identified as their fastest-selling market, with strong demand for luxury condos and high occupancy rates for rental projects.

articles.title: Great Central Brewing owners face second foreclosure, this time for nearly $5M on Near West Side
articles.narrative: The owners of Great Central Brewing Company are facing a $4.7 million foreclosure lawsuit on their Near West Side facility in Chicago, IL. Harvest Commercial Capital filed the suit against 221 Wood Holdings LLC, alleging default on a $4.75 million loan originated in December 2024. This marks the second foreclosure attempt on the property in recent years, highlighting the financial challenges within the craft brewery industry.

data_points.project_notes: The property was previously subject to a $12 million foreclosure lawsuit in 2023 by T2 Capital, which was later dismissed after the ownership recapitalized the asset. T2 Capital is still owed nearly $2 million and is named as a defendant in the current complaint. The Metropolitan Water Reclamation District of Greater Chicago holds over $52,000 in unresolved liens.

articles.market_intelligence: The craft brewery business in Chicagoland and nationally has faced challenges due to shifting consumer habits, rising material costs, and an oversaturated market, leading to downsizing, equipment sales, or closures for several local brewers.

articles.title: Domus’ Fulton Market apartment tower plan hits setback
articles.narrative: Chicago's City Council has postponed a vote on Domus Group's proposed 29-story, 347-unit residential tower in Fulton Market due to the developer's failure to finalize the purchase of land designated for a public park. The $150 million project, which seeks a zoning designation typically reserved for downtown skyscrapers, has faced controversy over its intensity and the precedent it might set for future developments in the area. The public park was a critical component that allowed the Chicago Plan Commission to advance the project despite initial zoning administrator recommendations against it.

data_points.notable_features: Proposed 29-story residential tower with a publicly accessible green space.
data_points.project_notes: Project requires a zoning designation typically reserved for downtown skyscrapers. The proposal is contingent on the developer completing the purchase of land for a public park at 1240 West Carroll Avenue.

articles.title: Reschke tees up 55 West Monroe purchase in latest Loop office bargain
articles.narrative: Prime Group, led by Mike Reschke, has acquired the 40-story office tower at 55 West Monroe Street in Chicago for approximately $25 million. The 815,000-square-foot building, which was previously purchased for $243 million in 2014, is currently over 80 percent vacant due to the impact of remote work. Reschke plans to maintain the property as a Class A office building for now, but is considering a future mixed-use conversion to include residential and hotel components, aligning with his strategy to revitalize the Loop through such developments.

data_points.notable_features: Helmut-Jahn designed building.
data_points.project_notes: Seller Manulife paid $243 million for the building in 2014.

articles.title: Multifamily momentum swells as three North Side Chicago complexes list
articles.narrative: Three prominent multifamily properties in Chicago's North Side have been listed for sale, signaling a potential uptick in the city's apartment market. The properties include the 310-unit Next tower in River North, the 324-unit SoNo East in Lincoln Park, and Eight Eleven in Uptown. Next, developed by Fifield Companies, is on the market for the first time with an asking price of $135 million. SoNo East, owned by PGIM Real Estate, is listed for $126 million, representing a significant increase over its 2012 acquisition price. All three properties are being marketed by Newmark.

data_points.notable_features: First time on the market since construction.
data_points.project_notes: Refinanced for $95 million in 2018.

articles.title: Hillwood moving low-and-slow with 1,000-acre master plan in Lockhart
articles.narrative: Hillwood Communities is progressing with a master-planned community on a 1,110-acre tract in Lockhart, Texas, located between Dry Creek Road and U.S. Highway 183. The development, which is primarily within Lockhart's extraterritorial jurisdiction, is moving at a deliberate pace due to an acreage expansion and ongoing municipal utility district approvals. This project aligns with a broader trend of developers targeting Austin's growing suburban markets.

data_points.notable_features: Located just south of the Lockhart city limits, in the extraterritorial jurisdiction.
data_points.project_notes: Developer requested a fourth 90-day extension for the proposed municipal utility district.

articles.title: Zom Living ordered to pay $323M after jury found it stole projects from former partner
articles.narrative: An Arizona jury has ordered Zom Living to pay Gray Development Group $323 million in compensatory damages after finding Zom liable for breach of contract. Gray Development Group, now defunct, alleged that Zom Living stole proprietary data and proceeded with five multifamily and commercial developments in Arizona independently, despite an initial partnership agreement. The projects, located in Phoenix and Scottsdale, were part of Gray's larger $1.4 billion development plan for the region.

data_points.notable_features: One of the projects is a 416-unit luxury multifamily venture within the Desert Ridge master-planned community.
data_points.project_notes: The dispute involved five projects, four of which have begun construction, and were part of Gray Development Group's decade-long $1.4 billion Arizona development plan involving 13 total projects.

articles.title: It’s official: Fifth Third Bank will exit Downtown Dallas Comerica Tower after Comerica merger
articles.narrative: Fifth Third Bank is vacating its 200,000-square-foot lease at Comerica Bank Tower in Downtown Dallas ahead of schedule, following its merger with Comerica. The bank will relocate to a new 63,000-square-foot space at a mixed-use development in Preston Center, with a full move-in anticipated in late 2028. This early departure, along with the upcoming expiration of Comerica's naming rights, creates uncertainty for the future of the Comerica Bank Tower, which is slated for a significant redevelopment.

data_points.notable_features: New mixed-use development featuring a 12-story, 300,000-square-foot office building and a 17-story residential tower.
data_points.project_notes: Fifth Third Bank is moving from its current 200,000-square-foot lease at 1717 Main Street, Dallas, TX. The bank will operate out of its North Park location at 8850 Boedeker Street until the new office is ready.

articles.market_intelligence: Downtown Dallas' office market is struggling, with AT&T's planned headquarters move to Plano leaving a 2 million-square-foot vacancy. The Comerica Bank Tower is planned for redevelopment to include 600,000 square feet of modern offices, 240 apartments, a 242-key hotel, and 10,000 square feet of ground floor retail.

articles.title: City leaders consider bolstering affordable housing fund to bring back developers
articles.narrative: San Francisco leaders are proposing a two-pronged strategy to reactivate the city's stalled housing development pipeline. One initiative involves a charter amendment to significantly increase the annual contribution to the city's Housing Trust Fund from $52 million to $125 million, extending its duration and linking future increases to property tax growth. Concurrently, the city is considering reducing inclusionary housing requirements for market-rate developers, lowering the affordable set-aside from 15% to 5% for projects with 25 or more units, or offering alternative compliance options. These measures aim to inject more public funding into affordable housing while reducing financial burdens on private developers to stimulate new construction.

data_points.project_notes: The Housing Trust Fund was established in 2012 and is currently set to expire in 2043. The proposed amendment would extend the fund through 2058.

companies_people.label: OWNER
companies_people.firm_name: City and County of San Francisco

companies_people.label: DEVELOPER/SPONSOR
companies_people.firm_name: City and County of San Francisco

articles.title: Sand Hill affiliate plots hundreds of apartments, townhomes in East Palo Alto
articles.narrative: An affiliate of Sand Hill Property Company, Woodland Park Communities, has submitted pre-applications for two significant residential projects in East Palo Alto, proposing nearly 800 new apartments and townhomes. The O’Keefe-Manhattan and West Bayshore-Newell Improvements projects aim to renovate existing apartments, construct new for-sale townhomes, and develop new apartment buildings across multiple sites. The developer, which has owned the properties since 2016, plans to avoid displacing current tenants by offering replacement rent-controlled units at stabilized rates, with formal applications anticipated this summer and construction phased over several years.

data_points.notable_features: The projects involve renovating existing apartments, constructing new for-sale townhomes, and developing new apartment buildings.
data_points.project_notes: The developer plans to avoid displacing current tenants by offering replacement rent-controlled units at stabilized rates. Woodland Park Communities has owned the properties since 2016 and has engaged in community meetings to shape the proposal. Formal applications are expected in summer 2026, with construction phased over multiple years.

articles.title: Affordable housing project rises from SiliconSage wreckage in Fremont
articles.narrative: A long-stalled development site in Fremont, previously associated with the collapse of SiliconSage Builders, has been acquired by USA Properties Fund and Riverside Charitable Corporation for $11.5 million. The developers plan to construct Centerville Plaza Apartments, a 290-unit fully affordable housing complex with ground-floor retail. The project will be built in two phases, with construction on the first phase expected to begin in June, following the anticipated closing of construction financing. The project is notable for its use of 4 percent low-income housing tax credits and a significant boost from the Housing Trust of Silicon Valley for its second phase.

data_points.notable_features: Fully affordable housing complex with ground-floor retail.
data_points.project_notes: The 2.9-acre site was previously approved for a mixed-use project by SiliconSage Builders in 2019, but the developer later imploded due to fraud allegations and filed for bankruptcy. Los Angeles-based Bolour Associates acquired the property through foreclosure. The project is being financed with 4 percent low-income housing tax credits, and the second phase will receive a $10.3 million boost from the Housing Trust of Silicon Valley. Phase 1 includes 140 apartments and 1,600 SF of retail; Phase 2 includes 150 apartments and 3,500 SF of retail.

articles.title: New York People and Company News, Week of May 22, 2026
articles.narrative: This article profiles Paul Bubny, Senior Content Director for Connect Commercial Real Estate, highlighting his extensive experience in commercial real estate journalism. It details his responsibilities, including overseeing daily operations, reporting on market trends and transactions, and developing longer-form content. His career spans over 30 years in business-to-business journalism, with 16 years specifically covering commercial real estate.

articles.title: Avison Young Markets Echo Park Multifamily Portfolio
articles.narrative: Avison Young has been selected to market for sale a newly constructed, Class A multifamily portfolio comprising 190 units across three properties in Los Angeles's Echo Park neighborhood. The portfolio, known as the Echo Park Portfolio, is being offered on behalf of seller Aragon Properties. The properties are exempt from the Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance, allowing for market-rate rent growth, which is a significant advantage in a market where most multifamily inventory is rent-controlled.

data_points.notable_features: Newly constructed, Class A multifamily portfolio exempt from Los Angeles Rent Stabilization Ordinance (RSO), allowing for unrestricted market-rate rent growth.
data_points.project_notes: The portfolio includes three properties: Park at 200 N Toluca St., Vista at 215 N Toluca St., and Zag at 1750 Glendale Blvd.

articles.title: Cain, Alchemy-ABR Secure $321M Refi for Newly Delivered Plaza District Offices
articles.narrative: Cain and Alchemy-ABR Investment Partners have secured a $321 million refinancing package for 125 W. 57th St., a recently completed Class A office tower located in Manhattan's Plaza District. The financing was provided by JPMorgan Chase as the senior lender and Hudson Bay Capital as the mezzanine lender. This refinancing underscores the strong demand for high-quality assets in prime locations, reflecting the project's exceptional design and execution.

data_points.address: 125 W. 57th St., New York City, NY
data_points.notable_features: Class A office tower located on Billionaires’ Row in Manhattan’s Plaza District.

articles.title: Marcus & Millichap Brokers $14M Sale, Financing of Indiana Property
articles.narrative: Marcus & Millichap facilitated the $14.1 million sale of The Villas at Green Valley, an 84-unit multifamily property in New Albany, Indiana. The buyer, a California-based investor, utilized a 1031 exchange to consolidate multiple California properties into this newer, larger community. Marcus & Millichap Capital Corporation also arranged $9.2 million in acquisition financing for the transaction.

data_points.address: 2412 Green Valley Rd, New Albany, IN
data_points.project_notes: Buyer completed a 1031 exchange, consolidating multiple California properties into this asset.

articles.financing: The loan features a 10-year term at 65% loan-to-value with a 5.85% interest rate and a 30-year amortization period.

articles.title: Landlords Are Turning Offices Into Art Galleries To Win Tenants
articles.narrative: Office landlords are increasingly investing millions in art installations and curated collections as a key strategy to attract and retain tenants, particularly in a competitive post-pandemic market. This trend moves beyond mere decoration, with landlords commissioning significant works and integrating art into building architecture to create 'enriched environments' that enhance employee productivity and engagement. The investment is proving effective, as prime office buildings with substantial renovations and art programs are achieving higher occupancy rates and better leasing figures compared to the broader market.

articles.title: Morgan Properties Hires Tech Czar To Deploy AI Across 110,000 Apartments
'Talk About Leverage': NYC Hoteliers Pay Dearly To Dodge World Cup Strike
articles.narrative: New York City hotel owners have agreed to a new bargaining agreement with the Hotel and Gaming Trades Council, averting a potential strike during the upcoming World Cup and America 250 celebrations. The deal, which is the most generous hotel labor agreement in the country, will see wages for unionized hotel workers increase by approximately 52% over eight years, reaching $60 an hour by 2034, in addition to continued full healthcare coverage. While this agreement provides predictability for investors and potential buyers, it is expected to significantly increase operating costs for hotel owners, who already face rising expenses and high occupancy taxes, likely leading to further upward pressure on room rates in the city's already expensive hotel market.

people.name: Chris Darling
people.title: Senior Analyst and Head of U.S. Lodging and Gaming Research

articles.title: 'Demand Has Gone Parabolic': Nvidia's Earnings Reveal AI Data Center Shifts
articles.article_type: Market Research / AI Data Center Trends
articles.narrative: Nvidia's latest earnings report highlights a significant shift in the AI data center landscape, driven by "parabolic" demand for AI computing. The company reported record quarterly revenue of $82 billion, an 85% year-over-year increase, primarily fueled by its data center segment which saw revenues nearly double to $75 billion. This surge is attributed to escalating AI infrastructure spending by major tech companies, with projections for capital expenditures to exceed $1 trillion by 2027, and the rapid growth of smaller AI-specific cloud providers, known as neoclouds. Neoclouds, which have tripled their revenue year-over-year, are emerging as crucial demand drivers by offering high-performance chips and Nvidia GPUs, often with faster market entry and better pricing than traditional hyperscalers, especially for inference computing workloads located near population centers.

articles.title: Choice Hotels International CEO Steps Down
articles.narrative: Patrick Pacious has stepped down as president and CEO of Choice Hotels International after leading the company since 2017 and serving for over two decades. Dominic Dragisich, Chief Growth and Strategy Officer, has been appointed interim CEO while the board conducts a search for a permanent replacement. The transition is described as amicable, with Pacious remaining in an advisory role through August. Choice Hotels reported strong first-quarter revenue, exceeding analyst estimates, despite slight year-over-year declines in revenue per available room, average daily rate, and occupancy.
